content: AD treatment includes symptomatic cognitive enhancers and new disease-modifying anti-amyloid therapies. Symptomatic: (1) Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) for mild-moderate AD; modest cognitive/functional benefit; side effects include nausea, diarrhea, bradycardia, syncope. Donepezil also approved for severe AD. (2) Memantine (NMDA receptor antagonist) for moderate-severe AD; can combine with cholinesterase inhibitor; side effects include dizziness, headache, confusion. Disease-modifying anti-amyloid monoclonal antibodies: lecanemab (Leqembi) and donanemab approved for early AD (MCI/mild dementia) with confirmed amyloid pathology. Lecanemab reduces amyloid and shows modest clinical benefit (0.45-point slowing on CDR-SB over 18 months); requires IV infusion every 2 weeks, MRI monitoring for ARIA (amyloid-related imaging abnormalities—edema/microhemorrhage, more common in APOE ε4 carriers). Donanemab targets deposited amyloid, treatment duration guided by amyloid PET clearance. ARIA risk requires baseline and serial MRIs, APOE genotyping recommended. Non-pharmacologic: cognitive stimulation, caregiver support, safety modifications, advance care planning. BPSD management: non-drug approaches first; antipsychotics only for severe agitation/psychosis (black box warning for mortality in dementia).
